dyed lifted polyps: The image shows a polyp elevated from the mucosal layer by a submucosal fluid injection, creating a 'lifted' appearance.
A blue contrast dye, such as indigo carmine, has been sprayed onto the polyp, highlighting its surface texture and pit pattern.
The photo displays the clear demarcation between the stained polypoid tissue and the surrounding normal mucosa.
A key feature is the submucosal cushion created by the injection, which enhances the safety margin for resection.
The dye pools within the mucosal pits and grooves, providing enhanced visualization of the lesion's micro-architecture.
This image confirms a successful 'non-lifting sign' is absent, as the lesion has risen uniformly with the injection.
The chromoendoscopy technique reveals the polyp's borders with high precision, which are now stained a deep blue.
Visible is a sessile polyp that now appears more protruded and well-defined after being lifted and dyed.
The staining accentuates the crypt opening patterns, aiding in the real-time optical diagnosis of the polyp.
This is an image of a lesion prepared for endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR), with the dye helping to ensure complete removal.
The lifted polyp exhibits a smooth surface with uniform dye uptake, suggesting benign characteristics.
In the photo, the base of the lifted polyp is clearly delineated by the pooling of the contrast agent.
The image illustrates how the dye enhances subtle mucosal irregularities on the surface of the elevated lesion.
This view shows the 'lift-and-dye' technique, used to assess the depth of invasion and resectability of a flat lesion.
The entire lesion is stained, indicating the dye was applied circumferentially to assess its full extent.
dyed resection margins: The image displays the post-resection site, with a ring of blue dye applied to the margins of the mucosal defect.
This photo shows a clean resection bed, with the dye clearly demarcating the edge of the removed tissue.
A key feature is the uniform staining of the margin, used to confirm the complete excision of a polyp or lesion.
The image shows no evidence of residual polypoid tissue within the dyed circumferential margin.
This is an image of a post-endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) site where chromoendoscopy is used to inspect for completeness.
The dye helps to meticulously inspect the tissue rim for any subtle abnormalities that may represent an incomplete resection.
Visible in the photo is the contrast between the white, coagulated resection base and the blue-stained mucosal edge.
The image confirms that the resection boundary has been clearly marked with dye for follow-up or histological correlation.
This photo shows the application of dye to assess the lateral margins immediately after the lesion was snared off.
The technique visualized here helps in reducing the local recurrence rate by ensuring no abnormal tissue is left behind.
The resection margin appears smooth and evenly stained, indicating a high likelihood of curative resection.
In the image, the underlying submucosa is visible at the center of the resection crater, surrounded by the dyed edge.
This view demonstrates a quality control step in therapeutic endoscopy, highlighting the importance of margin assessment.
The photo shows a well-defined circular defect from the resection, with its entire perimeter stained for inspection.
No suspicious, unstained mucosal islands are visible within or at the edge of the dyed resection margin.
esophagitis: The image shows linear erythematous streaks extending proximally from the gastroesophageal junction.
This photo displays one or more mucosal breaks, which are longitudinal erosions in the esophageal lining.
A key feature is diffuse erythema (redness) and friability of the esophageal mucosa, which bleeds on contact.
Visible in the image are confluent inflammatory lesions that occupy a significant portion of the esophageal circumference.
The photo shows whitish or yellowish exudates overlying the inflamed esophageal tissue.
This is an image of Grade C esophagitis, with mucosal breaks that are continuous between the tops of at least two mucosal folds.
The image reveals a loss of the normal, smooth, pearly-gray appearance of the squamous epithelium.
A circumferential mucosal break is visible in the distal esophagus, indicative of severe (Grade D) esophagitis.
The esophageal lumen appears narrowed due to severe inflammation and edema of the mucosal walls.
This image shows isolated, non-confluent erosions or 'islands' of inflammation in the distal esophagus.
The photo depicts a clear demarcation line of inflammation just above the Z-line.
In the image, the vascular pattern of the mucosa is obscured due to significant swelling and inflammation.
This scan reveals ulcerative lesions with well-defined borders against the surrounding inflamed mucosa.
The image shows a peptic stricture, a fibrotic narrowing of the esophagus resulting from chronic severe esophagitis.
This is a photo of the distal esophagus showing signs of healing esophagitis, such as re-epithelialization over previous erosions.
normal cecum: The image displays a panoramic view of the cecal pole, characterized by its wide, sac-like structure.
Clearly visible in the photo is the appendiceal orifice, appearing as a small, slit-like opening.
A key landmark in this image is the ileocecal valve, a protuberant structure with distinct lip-like folds.
The photo shows the convergence of the three taeniae coli, which merge at the base of the cecum.
This is an image of a healthy cecal mucosa, which is pale pink with a clearly visible, branching vascular pattern.
The image confirms a complete colonoscopy to the cecum by identifying these key anatomical landmarks.
The cecal lumen is well-distended and clean, with no evidence of polyps, inflammation, or diverticula.
The photo shows the triradiate fold pattern, where mucosal folds radiate from the appendiceal orifice.
In the image, the transition from the terminal ileum through the ileocecal valve into the cecum is identifiable.
This view shows a normal, compliant cecum without any signs of scarring, masses, or ulceration.
The image displays the characteristic haustral folds of the cecum, which are sacculations of the colon wall.
This photo demonstrates a retroflexed view in the cecum, showing the back of the ileocecal valve.
The mucosal surface is smooth and glistening, reflecting a healthy and well-hydrated bowel lining.
No abnormal pigmentation, erythema, or erosions are seen throughout the visualized cecal area.
The image confirms the absence of any pathological findings, representing a normal cecal examination.
normal pylorus: The image shows a round, symmetric, and patent pyloric orifice, which is the opening from the stomach to the duodenum.
This photo captures the dynamic, periodic opening and closing of the pylorus due to normal peristalsis.
A key feature is the smooth, pinkish mucosal surface of the surrounding gastric antrum, free of lesions.
The image displays a centrally located pyloric channel without any evidence of deformation or stenosis.
This is a photo of a normal pylorus, characterized by the absence of ulcerations, erythema, or scarring.
The pyloric ring appears concentric and pliable, readily admitting the passage of the endoscope into the duodenum.
In the image, the folds of the gastric antrum are seen converging symmetrically toward the pyloric opening.
This view shows the clear transition from the gastric mucosa to the villous pattern of the duodenal bulb as the pylorus opens.
The photo confirms that there is no 'clover-leaf' deformity or other signs of chronic ulcer disease.
The pylorus is free of any masses, polyps, or extrinsically compressing structures.
This image shows a normal, non-inflamed pyloric sphincter with a clear and unobstructed lumen.
The surrounding mucosa is intact, with no evidence of gastritis or bile reflux.
In the photo, the pylorus appears as a well-defined muscular ring at the distal end of the stomach.
This is an image showing a typical 'target' appearance as the pylorus begins to close.
The overall anatomy of the pyloric region is unremarkable, with no pathological findings.
normal z line: The image displays a sharp, regular, and well-demarcated Z-line, also known as the squamocolumnar junction.
This photo shows the distinct color transition from the pale, glossy squamous mucosa of the esophagus to the reddish, velvety columnar mucosa of the stomach.
A key feature is the circumferential and non-interrupted nature of the Z-line, located at the level of the gastroesophageal junction.
The image confirms the absence of any tongue-like protrusions or islands of salmon-colored mucosa extending above the junction, ruling out Barrett's esophagus.
This is a photo of a normal Z-line that is crisp and straight, without any signs of irregularity.
The junction is located precisely where the tubular esophagus meets the gastric folds of the stomach.
In the image, the fine blood vessels of the gastric mucosa are visible right up to the junction, contrasting with the less vascular esophageal mucosa.
This view shows a smooth and uniform transition between the two epithelial types.
The photo depicts a perfectly congruent Z-line that coincides with the anatomical gastroesophageal junction.
No signs of inflammation, erosion, or ulceration are present at or near the squamocolumnar junction.
The Z-line follows the contour of the distal esophagus without any upward displacement.
This is a textbook image of a normal Z-line, indicating no metaplastic changes.
The image clearly distinguishes the stratified squamous epithelium from the simple columnar epithelium.
This photo demonstrates the integrity of the gastroesophageal junction, a critical area for pathology.
The Z-line appears as a thin, symmetric, and unbroken circumferential line.
polyps: The image shows a sessile polyp, which is a flat, broad-based growth rising directly from the mucosal surface.
This photo displays a pedunculated polyp, a mushroom-like growth attached to the colon wall by a slender stalk.
A diminutive polyp, typically less than 5mm in size, is visible on a mucosal fold.
The image reveals a large, lobulated polyp with a cauliflower-like surface texture.
This photo shows a reddish, hyperemic polyp, suggesting high vascularity or inflammation.
A key feature is a flat polyp (non-polypoid lesion), which is subtly elevated and can be difficult to detect.
The surface of the polyp in the image has a distinct pit pattern, which can help predict its histology.
This is an image of a villous adenoma, characterized by its fine, finger-like projections on the surface.
The photo shows a polyp with central ulceration or depression, which can be a sign of advanced pathology.
A pale, smooth-surfaced polyp is seen, which is often characteristic of a hyperplastic polyp.
The image displays multiple polyps of varying sizes and shapes, a condition known as polyposis.
This is a photo of a tubular adenoma, typically appearing as a small, reddish, and slightly raised lesion.
In the image, a 'carpet-like' lesion is visible, representing a laterally spreading tumor.
The polyp is located in the sigmoid colon, one of the most common sites for colorectal polyps.
This image shows the use of narrow-band imaging (NBI) to enhance the vascular pattern on the polyp's surface for better characterization.
ulcerative colitis: The image shows diffuse, continuous erythema of the colonic mucosa, starting from the rectum.
A key feature is the complete loss of the normal honeycomb-like vascular pattern.
This photo displays a granular mucosal surface, which has a fine, sandpaper-like texture.
Visible in the image is mucosal friability, characterized by spontaneous bleeding or bleeding on light contact.
The photo shows multiple small, superficial ulcerations scattered across the inflamed mucosa.
This is an image of severe colitis with large, deep ulcers and spontaneous hemorrhage.
The colonic lumen is covered with a layer of mucopurulent exudate, a mixture of mucus and pus.
The image reveals the presence of pseudopolyps, which are islands of intact mucosa surrounded by ulcerated areas.
This photo demonstrates a lack of haustral folds, leading to a 'lead pipe' appearance in chronic disease.
The inflammation is circumferential and contiguous, without any 'skip lesions' of normal mucosa.
In the image, the boundary between the inflamed distal colon and the normal proximal colon is sharply demarcated.
This is a photo of proctitis, where inflammation is confined only to the rectum.
The mucosal surface is edematous and swollen, obscuring underlying anatomical details.
The image shows aphthous ulcers, which are small, shallow sores, representing an early sign of inflammation.
This view of the colon shows signs of active, moderate ulcerative colitis with erythema, erosions, and loss of vascularity.
